## Project Goal
Create an AI Project Manager that orchestrates multiple Claude agents to complete harness tasks in parallel, optimizing for quality, speed, and cost.

---

## Session 1 - 2025-12-16 - Harness Setup

### Context
User requested automation of the MMS harness with:
- AI acting as project manager
- Parallel task execution
- Dependency analysis
- Quality assurance

### Completed ✅
1. Created basic `scripts/harness-runner.js` - sequential runner (committed: `a571f97`)
2. Created `packages/harness-orchestrator/` - isolated package (committed: `0aab9bf`)
3. Defined features.json with 10 features (O001-O010)
4. Created CLAUDE.md with architecture and instructions
5. Created NOTES.md with technical learnings pattern

### Architecture Decisions

#### Why Isolated Package?
- Avoid dependency conflicts with main project
- Can install own deps (commander, chalk, ora)
- Separate harness tracking
- Won't interfere with parallel agents on main project

#### Core Components Planned
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ORCHESTRATOR                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐  │
│  │  Dependency  │───▶│    Task      │───▶│    Agent     │  │
│  │    Graph     │    │  Scheduler   │    │    Pool      │  │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘  │
│         │                   │                   │           │
│         └───────────────────┼───────────────────┘           │
│                             ▼                               │
│                    ┌──────────────┐                         │
│                    │    State     │                         │
│                    │   Manager    │                         │
│                    └──────────────┘                         │
│                             │                               │
│                             ▼                               │
│                    ┌──────────────┐                         │
│                    │     AI       │                         │
│                    │   Manager    │                         │
│                    └──────────────┘                         │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### Parallelization Strategy
1. Build DAG from features.json blockedBy
2. Find all nodes with no pending dependencies (ready queue)
3. Execute ready tasks in parallel (up to max-agents limit)
4. On completion: update state, unlock dependents, repeat

#### Critical Path Optimization
- Calculate longest path through DAG
- Prioritize tasks on critical path
- Reduces total completion time

### Current State
| Feature | Status | Notes |
|---------|--------|-------|
| O001 | ❌ Pending | Package initialization |
| O002 | ❌ Pending | Dependency graph (blocked by O001) |
| O003 | ❌ Pending | Task scheduler (blocked by O002) |
| O004 | ❌ Pending | Agent pool (blocked by O001) |
| O005 | ❌ Pending | State manager (blocked by O001) |
| O006 | ❌ Pending | Main orchestrator (blocked by O002-O005) |
| O007 | ❌ Pending | AI manager (blocked by O006) |
| O008 | ❌ Pending | CLI interface (blocked by O006) |
| O009 | ❌ Pending | Dashboard (blocked by O008) |
| O010 | ❌ Pending | Verification system (blocked by O006) |

### Parallelization Analysis
After O001 completes, these can run in parallel:
- O002 (Dependency graph)
- O004 (Agent pool)
- O005 (State manager)

This is a 3x speedup opportunity!

### Key Files
| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `features.json` | Feature definitions and status |
| `progress.txt` | This session log |
| `CLAUDE.md` | AI instructions and architecture |
| `NOTES.md` | Technical learnings and gotchas |

- Avoid dependency conflicts with main project
- Can install own deps (commander, chalk, ora)
- Separate harness tracking
- Won't interfere with parallel agents on main project

#### Core Components Planned
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ORCHESTRATOR                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐  │

│  │    Graph     │    │  Scheduler   │    │    Pool      │  │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘  │
│         │                   │                   │           │
│         └───────────────────┼───────────────────┘           │
│                             ▼                               │
│                    ┌──────────────┐                         │
│                    │    State     │                         │
│                    │   Manager    │                         │
│                    └──────────────┘                         │
│                             │                               │
│                             ▼                               │
│                    ┌──────────────┐                         │
│                    │     AI       │                         │
│                    │   Manager    │                         │
│                    └──────────────┘                         │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### Parallelization Strategy
1. Build DAG from features.json blockedBy
2. Find all nodes with no pending dependencies (ready queue)
3. Execute ready tasks in parallel (up to max-agents limit)
4. On completion: update state, unlock dependents, repeat

- Calculate longest path through DAG
- Prioritize tasks on critical path
- Reduces total completion time

# Orchestrator - Technical Notes

Notes, gotchas, and solutions discovered during development. Read this to avoid repeating past mistakes.

---

## Claude Code CLI Notes

### Key Learning
The `claude` CLI has different modes that affect behavior significantly.

#### Modes
| Mode | Flag | Use Case |
|------|------|----------|
| Interactive | (default) | Human conversation, debugging |
| Print | `-p` | Scripted execution, single task |
| Continue | `--continue` | Resume last session |
| Resume | `--resume <id>` | Resume specific session |

#### Important Options
```bash
# Non-interactive execution (for automation)
claude -p "prompt"

# Limit spending
claude --max-budget-usd 5 -p "prompt"

# Specify model
claude --model sonnet -p "prompt"

# Output format for parsing
claude -p --output-format json "prompt"
```

---

## Parallelization Notes

### Key Learning
Not all "independent" tasks are safe to parallelize.

#### Safe to Parallelize
- Tasks that modify different files
- Tasks that read but don't write shared state
- Tasks with no file overlap

#### NOT Safe to Parallelize
- Tasks that both modify features.json
- Tasks that might conflict in git
- Tasks that share build/test infrastructure

#### Solution: File Locking
```javascript
// Use proper-lockfile for atomic updates
import { lock } from 'proper-lockfile';

const release = await lock('features.json');
try {
  // Read, modify, write
} finally {
  await release();
}
```

---

## Process Management Notes

### Key Learning
Child processes need careful handling.

#### Correct Usage
```javascript
const claude = spawn('claude', args, {
  cwd: ROOT_DIR,
  stdio: 'inherit', // Show output in terminal
  shell: true,      // Required for some environments
});

// Handle all events
claude.on('error', handleError);
claude.on('close', handleClose);
claude.on('exit', handleExit);
```

#### Common Pitfalls
- Don't assume process exits cleanly - always handle errors
- Use `shell: true` for PATH resolution
- Capture stderr separately if you need error logs
- Set timeouts to prevent hanging processes

---

## DAG/Dependency Notes

### Key Learning
The `blockedBy` field can be a string or array.

#### Correct Parsing
```javascript
function getDependencies(feature) {
  if (!feature.blockedBy) return [];
  return Array.isArray(feature.blockedBy)
    ? feature.blockedBy
    : [feature.blockedBy];
}
```

#### Cycle Detection
A harness with cycles will deadlock. Always validate:
```javascript
function detectCycles(graph) {
  const visited = new Set();
  const stack = new Set();

  function dfs(node) {
    visited.add(node);
    stack.add(node);

    for (const dep of graph.getDependents(node)) {
      if (stack.has(dep)) return true; // Cycle!
      if (!visited.has(dep) && dfs(dep)) return true;
    }

    stack.delete(node);
    return false;
  }

  return [...graph.nodes].some(n => !visited.has(n) && dfs(n));
}
```

---

## Error Solutions

### Error: "ENOENT: no such file or directory" on file lock
**Cause**: Trying to lock a file that doesn't exist
**Solution**: Create file first or use `realpath: false` option

### Error: Child process hangs indefinitely
**Cause**: Claude waiting for input or stuck in loop
**Solution**: Add timeout and kill on timeout
```javascript
const timeout = setTimeout(() => {
  claude.kill('SIGTERM');
}, 10 * 60 * 1000); // 10 minutes

claude.on('close', () => clearTimeout(timeout));
```

### Error: features.json corrupted after parallel writes
**Cause**: Race condition - two processes read/write simultaneously
**Solution**: Use file locking (proper-lockfile)

### Error: Git merge conflicts from parallel branches
**Cause**: Multiple agents committing to same branch
**Solution**: Use feature branches, merge sequentially

---

## Performance Notes

### Optimal Agent Count
| System | Recommended | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| Local dev | 2-3 | CPU bound |
| CI/CD | 3-5 | I/O bound |
| API limits | Check rate limits | Anthropic has per-minute limits |

### Cost Optimization
- Use `haiku` for simple/quick tasks
- Use `sonnet` for normal tasks
- Reserve `opus` for complex/critical tasks

---

## Useful Commands

```bash
# Test orchestrator locally
node packages/harness-orchestrator/src/index.js analyze features.json

# Check for pending tasks
jq '.features[] | select(.passes == false) | .id' features.json

# Find blocked features
jq '.features[] | select(.blockedBy != null and .passes == false)' features.json

# Count parallelizable tasks
node -e "
  const f = require('./features.json');
  const ready = f.features.filter(t =>
    !t.passes &&
    (!t.blockedBy || f.features.find(b => b.id === t.blockedBy)?.passes)
  );
  console.log('Ready:', ready.map(t => t.id));
"
```

---

## Resources

- [Claude Code CLI Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/claude-code)
- [proper-lockfile npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/proper-lockfile)
- [DAG algorithms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Directed_acyclic_graph)
- [Process management in Node.js](https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html)

# Harness Orchestrator - Progress Log

## Project Goal
Create an AI Project Manager that orchestrates multiple Claude agents to complete harness tasks in parallel, optimizing for quality, speed, and cost.

---

## Session 1 - 2025-12-16 - Harness Setup

### Context
User requested automation of the MMS harness with:
- AI acting as project manager
- Parallel task execution
- Dependency analysis
- Quality assurance

### Completed ✅
1. Created basic `scripts/harness-runner.js` - sequential runner (committed: `a571f97`)
2. Created `packages/harness-orchestrator/` - isolated package (committed: `0aab9bf`)
3. Defined features.json with 10 features (O001-O010)
4. Created CLAUDE.md with architecture and instructions
5. Created NOTES.md with technical learnings pattern

### Architecture Decisions

#### Why Isolated Package?
- Avoid dependency conflicts with main project
- Can install own deps (commander, chalk, ora)
- Separate harness tracking
- Won't interfere with parallel agents on main project

#### Core Components Planned
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ORCHESTRATOR                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐  │
│  │  Dependency  │───▶│    Task      │───▶│    Agent     │  │
│  │    Graph     │    │  Scheduler   │    │    Pool      │  │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘  │
│         │                   │                   │           │
│         └───────────────────┼───────────────────┘           │
│                             ▼                               │
│                    ┌──────────────┐                         │
│                    │    State     │                         │
│                    │   Manager    │                         │
│                    └──────────────┘                         │
│                             │                               │
│                             ▼                               │
│                    ┌──────────────┐                         │
│                    │     AI       │                         │
│                    │   Manager    │                         │
│                    └──────────────┘                         │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### Parallelization Strategy
1. Build DAG from features.json blockedBy
2. Find all nodes with no pending dependencies (ready queue)
3. Execute ready tasks in parallel (up to max-agents limit)
4. On completion: update state, unlock dependents, repeat

#### Critical Path Optimization
- Calculate longest path through DAG
- Prioritize tasks on critical path
- Reduces total completion time

### Current State
| Feature | Status | Notes |
|---------|--------|-------|
| O001 | ❌ Pending | Package initialization |
| O002 | ❌ Pending | Dependency graph (blocked by O001) |
| O003 | ❌ Pending | Task scheduler (blocked by O002) |

| O009 | ❌ Pending | Dashboard (blocked by O008) |
| O010 | ❌ Pending | Verification system (blocked by O006) |

### Parallelization Analysis
After O001 completes, these can run in parallel:
- O002 (Dependency graph)
- O004 (Agent pool)
- O005 (State manager)

This is a 3x speedup opportunity!

### Key Files
| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `features.json` | Feature definitions and status |
| `progress.txt` | This session log |
| `CLAUDE.md` | AI instructions and architecture |
| `NOTES.md` | Technical learnings and gotchas |

### Learnings from Main Project
Applied patterns from MMS main project:
1. ✅ Structured progress log with session markers
2. ✅ Technical notes file (NOTES.md) for gotchas
3. ✅ Current state table at end of each session
4. ✅ "Initial Approach (Failed)" → "Final Solution" pattern
5. ✅ Explicit file lists with purposes

### Next Session Priorities
1. **O001**: Initialize package (package.json, tsconfig, src/)
2. **O002 + O004 + O005**: Can parallelize after O001!

---

Extract all sandboxing options for Claude Code, including Docker, macOS sandbox, and any other isolation methods. Include commands and configuration.

content.content:
# Claude Code Sandboxing Options

## Overview

Claude Code provides native sandboxing with OS-level filesystem and network isolation. The sandboxed bash tool supports two platforms:

### **Linux: Bubblewrap**
- Uses [bubblewrap](https://github.com/containers/bubblewrap) for isolation
- Includes an `enableWeakerNestedSandbox` mode for Docker environments (without privileged namespaces)
- Provides strong filesystem and network isolation in standard mode

### **macOS: Seatbelt**
- Uses Apple's Seatbelt sandbox enforcement
- OS-level security primitives for process isolation

## Sandbox Modes

### 1. **Auto-allow Mode**
- Bash commands run inside sandbox automatically without permission prompts
- Commands requiring network access to non-allowed hosts fall back to regular permission flow
- Explicit ask/deny rules are always respected
- Works independently of permission mode settings

### 2. **Regular Permissions Mode**
- All bash commands go through standard permission flow
- Requires explicit approval even when sandboxed
- Provides more control with more approvals needed

## Enabling Sandboxing

```bash
/sandbox
```

This opens a menu to choose between sandbox modes.

## Configuration

Configure sandboxing in `settings.json`:

```json
{
  "sandbox": {
    "network": {
      "httpProxyPort": 8080,
      "socksProxyPort": 8081
    }
  }
}
```

See [Settings documentation](/docs/en/settings#sandbox-settings) for complete reference.

## Isolation Features

### **Filesystem Isolation**
- **Default writes**: Current working directory and subdirectories
- **Default reads**: Entire computer (except denied directories)
- **Modifications**: Cannot modify files outside working directory without explicit permission
- **Configurable**: Define custom allowed/denied paths

### **Network Isolation**
- Domain restrictions via proxy server
- User confirmation for new domain requests
- Custom proxy support for advanced users
- Comprehensive coverage including all child processes

## Security Limitations & Considerations

- **Network filtering**: Doesn't inspect traffic; users responsible for allowing only trusted domains
- **Domain fronting**: Possible bypass through domain fronting techniques
- **Unix sockets** (`allowUnixSockets`): Can grant access to powerful system services (e.g., Docker socket)
- **Filesystem permissions**: Overly broad write permissions enable privilege escalation
- **Linux weaker mode**: `enableWeakerNestedSandbox` considerably weakens security

## Incompatible Tools

```bash
# watchman incompatible - use this for jest:
jest --no-watchman

# docker incompatible - exclude from sandbox:
# Set in settings.json: "excludedCommands": ["docker"]
```

## Escape Hatch Mechanism

Commands can use `dangerouslyDisableSandbox` parameter when sandbox constraints prevent execution. These go through normal permissions flow requiring user approval.

**Disable escape hatch:**
```json
{
  "sandbox": {
    "allowUnsandboxedCommands": false
  }
}
```

When disabled, `dangerouslyDisableSandbox` is completely ignored.

## Open Source Runtime

Available as an npm package for custom agent projects:

```bash
npx @anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime <command-to-sandbox>
```

Source: [GitHub repository](https://github.com/anthropic-experimental/sandbox-runtime)

## CLI Reference

Use the `-sb` flag for sandbox-related command-line options. See [CLI reference](/docs/en/cli-reference) for complete details.

# Complete Guide to Running Claude Code in Docker Sandbox

## Quick Start

The most straightforward approach to launch Claude in a sandboxed environment:

```bash
$ docker sandbox run claude
```

This initiates a sandboxed Claude Code agent using your current working directory as the workspace. To designate a different workspace location:

```bash
$ docker sandbox run -w ~/my-project claude
```

## Passing CLI Options

Claude Code accepts various command-line arguments that flow through `docker sandbox run`. Arguments following the agent identifier (`claude`) transfer directly to Claude Code within the sandbox.

### Resume Previous Conversation

To continue your most recent session:

```bash
$ docker sandbox run claude -c
```

Or using the extended format:

```bash
$ docker sandbox run claude --continue
```

### Direct Prompt Input

Begin Claude with an immediate instruction:

```bash
$ docker sandbox run claude "Add error handling to the login function"
```

### Option Combinations

Mix sandbox parameters with Claude parameters:

```bash
$ docker sandbox run -e DEBUG=1 claude -c
```

This establishes a sandbox with debugging enabled and resumes your previous conversation.

### Available Parameters

Key Claude Code options include:
- `-c, --continue` — Resume the most recent conversation
- `-p, --prompt` — Receive prompt via stdin for piping operations
- `--dangerously-skip-permissions` — Bypass permission dialogs (enabled by default)

## Authentication Strategies

### Strategy 1: Sandbox (Default)

```bash
$ docker sandbox run claude
```

Upon initial execution, Claude requests your Anthropic API key. Credentials store in a persistent Docker volume called `docker-claude-sandbox-data`. Future sandboxes automatically leverage these stored credentials. The volume mounts at `/mnt/claude-data` with symbolic links established in the sandbox user's home directory.

**Important note:** "If your workspace contains a `.claude.json` file with a `primaryApiKey` field, you'll receive a warning about potential conflicts."

### Strategy 2: None

```bash
$ docker sandbox run --credentials=none claude
```

Docker performs no automatic credential discovery or injection. Manual authentication occurs within the container, with credentials remaining isolated to that specific sandbox instance.

## Configuration

Pass Claude-specific options following the agent identifier:

```bash
$ docker sandbox run claude [claude-options]
```

Example:

```bash
$ docker sandbox run claude --continue
```

## Base Image Details

The `docker/sandbox-templates:claude-code` image includes:
- Claude Code with automatic credential management
- Development utilities (Docker CLI, GitHub CLI, Node.js, Go, Python 3, Git, ripgrep, jq)
- Non-root `agent` user with `sudo` privileges
- Pre-configured `--dangerously-skip-permissions` flag

## Next Steps

Explore advanced configurations including environment variables, volume mounts, and Docker socket access in the [Advanced configurations](https://docs.docker.com/ai/sandboxes/advanced-config/) documentation.

# Claude Code Sandbox - README Summary

## Overview

Claude Code Sandbox enables running Claude Code as an autonomous agent within isolated Docker containers, eliminating the need for permission approvals. The project allows AI-assisted development with automatic GitHub integration while maintaining security through containerization.

## Installation

Install globally via npm:
```bash
npm install -g @textcortex/claude-code-sandbox
```

**Prerequisites:**
- Node.js >= 18.0.0
- Docker or Podman
- Git
- Claude Code (`npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@latest`)

## Key Usage Commands

**Start a new session:**
```bash
claude-sandbox
```
This creates a new branch, launches a Docker container, and opens a web UI at `http://localhost:3456`.

**Additional commands:**
- `claude-sandbox start [options]` - Explicitly start with configuration
- `claude-sandbox attach [container-id]` - Connect to existing container
- `claude-sandbox list` - View all running containers
- `claude-sandbox stop [container-id]` - Halt containers
- `claude-sandbox logs [container-id]` - View container output
- `claude-sandbox clean` - Remove stopped containers
- `claude-sandbox config` - Display current settings

## How It Works

The sandbox operates by:

1. **Creating isolated environments** - Each session spawns a dedicated Docker container with the full repository copied inside
2. **Enabling autonomous execution** - Claude runs with the `--dangerously-skip-permissions` flag safely within containers
3. **Monitoring commits** - Changes trigger notifications displaying syntax-highlighted diffs
4. **Managing workflows** - Users review changes and choose to push branches or create pull requests

## Configuration

Create `claude-sandbox.config.json`:

```json
{
  "autoStartClaude": true,
  "autoPush": true,
  "autoCreatePR": true,
  "setupCommands": ["npm install"],
  "environment": {"NODE_ENV": "development"}
}
```

## Notable Features

- **Credential discovery** - Automatically forwards Anthropic API keys, GitHub tokens, and git configurations
- **Podman support** - Works with both Docker and Podman containers
- **Web UI terminal** - Browser-based interface for monitoring Claude's work
- **Multiple concurrent sessions** - Run several isolated instances simultaneously
- **Custom Dockerfiles** - Extend the default image with project-specific tools

The tool creates isolated branches for each session, preserving git history while preventing accidental modifications to main branches.

npm install -g @textcortex/claude-code-sandbox
```

**Prerequisites:**
- Node.js >= 18.0.0
- Docker or Podman
- Git
- Claude Code (`npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@latest`)

## Key Usage Commands

**Start a new session:**
```bash
claude-sandbox
```
This creates a new branch, launches a Docker container, and opens a web UI at `http://localhost:3456`.

**Additional commands:**
- `claude-sandbox start [options]` - Explicitly start with configuration
- `claude-sandbox attach [container-id]` - Connect to existing container
- `claude-sandbox list` - View all running containers
- `claude-sandbox stop [container-id]` - Halt containers
- `claude-sandbox logs [container-id]` - View container output
- `claude-sandbox clean` - Remove stopped containers
- `claude-sandbox config` - Display current settings

## How It Works

The sandbox operates by:

1. **Creating isolated environments** - Each session spawns a dedicated Docker container with the full repository copied inside
2. **Enabling autonomous execution** - Claude runs with the `--dangerously-skip-permissions` flag safely within containers
3. **Monitoring commits** - Changes trigger notifications displaying syntax-highlighted diffs
4. **Managing workflows** - Users review changes and choose to push branches or create pull requests

## Configuration

Create `claude-sandbox.config.json`:

```json
{
  "autoStartClaude": true,
  "autoPush": true,
  "autoCreatePR": true,
  "setupCommands": ["npm install"],
  "environment": {"NODE_ENV": "development"}
}
```

## Notable Features

- **Credential discovery** - Automatically forwards Anthropic API keys, GitHub tokens, and git configurations
- **Podman support** - Works with both Docker and Podman containers
- **Web UI terminal** - Browser-based interface for monitoring Claude's work
- **Multiple concurrent sessions** - Run several isolated instances simultaneously
- **Custom Dockerfiles** - Extend the default image with project-specific tools

The tool creates isolated branches for each session, preserving git history while preventing accidental modifications to main branches.

## Sim! Existem várias opções oficiais e da comunidade

### 1. **Docker Sandbox Oficial** (Recomendado)
A Anthropic oferece suporte nativo via Docker:

```bash
# Instalar Docker Sandbox
docker extension install docker/ai-sandbox

# Executar Claude Code em sandbox
docker sandbox run claude

# Com diretório específico
docker sandbox run -w ~/meu-projeto claude

# Continuar sessão anterior
docker sandbox run claude --continue

# Com prompt direto
docker sandbox run claude "Implemente feature X"
```

**Características:**
- `--dangerously-skip-permissions` habilitado por padrão
- Credenciais persistidas em volume Docker
- Inclui: Node.js, Python, Go, Git, GitHub CLI
- [Documentação oficial](https://docs.docker.com/ai/sandboxes/claude-code/)

---

### 2. **Sandbox Nativo** (macOS/Linux)
Claude Code tem sandboxing built-in:

```bash
# Ativar modo sandbox
/sandbox
```

**Plataformas:**
- **Linux**: Usa [Bubblewrap](https://github.com/containers/bubblewrap)
- **macOS**: Usa Apple Seatbelt

**Runtime standalone:**
```bash
npx @anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime <comando>
```

[Documentação de Sandboxing](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing)

---

### 3. **Claude Code Sandbox** (Comunidade)
Solução popular com Web UI:

```bash
npm install -g @textcortex/claude-code-sandbox

# Iniciar sessão
claude-sandbox

# Web UI em http://localhost:3456
```

**Features:**
- Cria branch isolada por sessão
- Notificações de commits com diffs
- Suporte a múltiplas sessões paralelas
- [GitHub: textcortex/claude-code-sandbox](https://github.com/textcortex/claude-code-sandbox)

---

### 4. **Para o Nosso Orchestrator**

Para testar o harness-orchestrator, podemos usar:

```bash
# Opção A: Docker Sandbox oficial
docker sandbox run -w /caminho/projeto claude -p "
  cd packages/harness-orchestrator
  cat features.json
  # Execute feature O002
"

# Opção B: Headless com skip-permissions
claude --dangerously-skip-permissions \
  --allowedTools "Bash(git*)" "Read" "Write" "Edit" \
  -p "Implement feature O002 from features.json"
```
